title: What Does My Mortality Awareness Change About Today?
subtitle: A journal prompt for using the awareness of finitude as a clarifying lens
excerpt: A journal prompt for using mortality awareness as a practical lens on today. Not morbid, but specific: if this decade is finite, what does that actually change about how you spend the hours in it?
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on Stoic and existentialist traditions of using memento mori as a clarifying practice.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to clarify what actually matters to you, or when the awareness of time being finite is present and you want to work with it rather than push it away.

content.text: The Stoics used the awareness of death deliberately: not to generate fear but to generate clarity. When you know that time is finite, the question of how to spend it becomes more urgent and more specific. This prompt uses that awareness as a lens on today, not as an abstraction.
Set a timer for fifteen minutes. Write without stopping. Begin with the prompt below.
What does my mortality awareness change about today?

If you need an entry point, try: If I knew this decade had a fixed number of years left in it, what would I spend less time worrying about? What would I stop postponing? What would I stop tolerating? What would I do more of, starting today? Who do I want to have spent time with?

The point of this prompt is not fear. It is clarity. The awareness of finitude is one of the sharpest tools available for cutting through what does not actually matter. What you write here is not a list of things to feel bad about. It is a map of what to move toward.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on Stoic practice and the idea that death awareness clarifies what matters.
